sources:
L Like Traditional Software: ML systems have unique dependencies on data and 
model behavior that traditional software engineering practices do not account for. Teams that 
ignore these differences find ML systems much harder to maintain, debug, and improve. 
 
Key Takeaways 
• 
MLOps is essential for deploying and managing machine learning systems in production at scale 
• 
The full lifecycle matters, not just model development — data, training, deployment, monitoring, 
and retraining are equally important 
• 
MLOps bridges the gap between ML experimentation and real-world production applications 
• 
Automation and CI/CD ensure reliability, consistency, and speed in model delivery 
• 
Continuous feedback loops help maintain and improve model performance over time 
• 
Cross-functional collaboration across data science, engineering, and operations teams is critical 
for MLOps success 
Project Overview 
 
 
© Copyright. upGrad Education Pvt. Ltd. All Rights Reserved. 
 
 
This textbook e

estration, the Vertex Feature Store for 
centralized feature management, and integrated monitoring and explainability tools. 
Azure Machine Learning: Azure ML provides enterprise-grade MLOps capabilities including experiment 
tracking, the Azure ML Model Registry, automated ML pipelines, and integration with Azure DevOps for 
CI/CD workflows. 
Tools by MLOps Stage 
Stage 
Popular Tools 
Experiment Tracking 
MLflow, Weights & Biases (W&B), Neptune.ai 
Data and Model Versioning 
DVC (Data Version Control), Delta Lake, LakeFS 
Model Registry 
MLflow Model Registry, SageMaker Model Registry 
Pipeline / Workflow 
Apache Airflow, Kubeflow Pipelines, ZenML 
Deployment 
FastAPI, BentoML, Seldon Core, KServe 
Orchestration 
Kubernetes, Kubeflow, Metaflow 
Monitoring 
Evidently AI, WhyLabs, Arize AI, Prometheus + Grafana

• 
Poor Collaboration: In many organisations, data scientists, ML engineers, and operations teams 
work in silos, using different tools and conventions. MLOps provides shared infrastructure, 
workflows, and conventions that enable seamless collaboration. 
 
Business Value of MLOps 
Adopting MLOps delivers measurable business outcomes: 
• 
Faster time-to-production: Automated pipelines reduce model deployment time from weeks to 
hours 
• 
Reduced operational risk: Systematic monitoring and validation prevent silent model failures 
• 
Improved model quality: Continuous retraining ensures models remain accurate as data evolves 
• 
Cost efficiency: Automation reduces the manual effort required to maintain production models 
 
The MLOps Lifecycle 
The MLOps lifecycle is a closed-loop system. Unlike traditional software, it never truly "finishes" —

© Copyright. upGrad Education Pvt. Ltd. All Rights Reserved. 
 
 
MLOps Lifecycle 
Introduction to MLOps 
MLOps (Machine Learning Operations) is a set of practices that automates and manages the end-to-end 
machine learning lifecycle; from data preparation to model deployment and ongoing monitoring. The term 
combines "Machine Learning" with "Operations," reflecting its roots in the DevOps philosophy of bridging 
development and deployment workflows. 
At its core, MLOps aims to: 
• 
Standardise and automate ML workflows 
• 
Ensure reproducibility across experiments and environments 
• 
Enable collaboration between data scientists, ML engineers, and operations teams 
• 
Maintain model reliability and performance after deployment 
 
MLOps vs. DevOps 
While MLOps borrows heavily from DevOps principles, it introduces unique challenges due to the data-
driven and experimental nature of machine learning. 
Dimension 
DevOps 
MLOps 
Primary focus 
Code deployment 
Code, data, and model man

quent deployments without 
proportionally increasing headcount. 
• 
Reduced Risk: Systematic testing, monitoring, and rollback capabilities minimize errors, reduce 
production failures, and improve the overall reliability of ML systems. 
 
MLOps Adoption Maturity 
Levels of MLOps Maturity 
Organizations do not adopt MLOps overnight. They typically evolve through three maturity levels: 
• 
Level 1 — Manual Process 
Model development, deployment, and monitoring are handled manually with minimal 
automation. Data scientists train models in notebooks, hand off artifacts to engineers, and 
models are deployed through ad hoc scripts. There is no systematic monitoring or versioning. 
• 
Level 2 — Partial Automation 
Some stages (training, testing, or deployment) are automated, but workflows are not fully 
integrated. Teams begin adopting tools for experiment tracking and model versioning, but end-to-
end pipelines are still fragmented. 
• 
Level 3 — Fully Automated Pipelines 
End-to-end pipel

te manual handoffs and reduce 
deployment time from weeks to hours. 
 
 
© Copyright. upGrad Education Pvt. Ltd. All Rights Reserved. 
 
 
• 
Better Collaboration: Shared pipelines, version-controlled assets, and standardized tooling 
improve collaboration between data scientists, engineers, and operations teams. Teams can 
work concurrently on different stages of the ML lifecycle without stepping on each other's work. 
• 
Reproducibility: MLOps ensures that experiments, data, and models can be consistently 
reproduced across environments and team members. This is essential for scientific rigor, 
debugging, and regulatory compliance. 
• 
Scalability: MLOps provides the infrastructure and automation needed to scale machine learning 
operations — handling more models, more data, and more frequent deployments without 
proportionally increasing headcount. 
• 
Reduced Risk: Systematic testing, monitoring, and rollback capabilities minimize errors, reduce 
production failures, and improve th

s the manual effort required to maintain production models 
 
The MLOps Lifecycle 
The MLOps lifecycle is a closed-loop system. Unlike traditional software, it never truly "finishes" — 
models must be continuously evaluated, retrained, and redeployed to maintain their value. 
The key stages of the MLOps lifecycle are: 
1. Data Engineering and Feature Management 
2. Experiment Tracking and Model Training 
3. CI/CD and Model Registry 
4. Model Deployment 
5. Model Monitoring 
6. Retraining and Continuous Improvement 
 
 
 
© Copyright. upGrad Education Pvt. Ltd. All Rights Reserved. 
 
 
MLOps Architecture 
The MLOps architecture connects all lifecycle stages through automated pipelines: 
 
Each stage feeds into the next, and monitoring outputs feed back into the retraining stage, creating a 
continuous improvement loop. 
 
Data Engineering and Feature Management 
The Foundation of Every Model 
 
 
© Copyright. upGrad Education Pvt. Ltd. All Rights Reserved. 
 
 
A model is only as good

s start with a "Traditional ML" approach — a model is trained once, deployed manually, 
and rarely revisited. MLOps transforms this into a continuous, automated lifecycle.

Continuous lifecycle

Automated pipelines 
Monitoring

Continuous monitoring

Continuous retraining 
 
The transition from Traditional ML to MLOps is not just a tooling change — it represents a cultural and 
organisational shift in how teams think about model development and maintenance. 
 
The Problems MLOps Solves 
Without MLOps, organisations encounter a predictable set of failures as they try to scale machine 
learning from experiments to production systems. 
• 
Lack of Reproducibility: Data scientists frequently struggle to reproduce model results. Without 
versioned data, tracked experiments, and documented environments, reproducing a model 
becomes nearl

MLOps deals with challenges that DevOps does not face: data drift handling, model retraining, and 
experiment tracking. These require additional tooling, processes, and governance practices.

Traditional ML vs. MLOps 
Many organisations start with a "Traditional ML" approach — a model is trained once, deployed manually, 
and rarely revisited. MLOps transforms this into a continuous, automated lifecycle.

, Prometheus + Grafana 
 
 
Key Challenges in MLOps 
 
 
© Copyright. upGrad Education Pvt. Ltd. All Rights Reserved. 
 
 
Understanding MLOps Challenges 
Implementing MLOps at scale is not straightforward. Organizations face both technical and 
organizational challenges that require deliberate strategies to overcome. 
Challenge 
Description 
Model Drift 
Gradual degradation in model performance over time as real-world data distributions shift 
Data Inconsistencies 
Variations in data quality and distribution across environments and time periods 
Reproducibility Issues 
Difficulty in replicating experiments and results across team members and environments 
Deployment Delays 
Slow and complex model release processes caused by manual steps and inadequate tooling 
Lack of Collaboration 
Gaps and friction between data science, engineering, and operations teams 
 
Strategies for Overcoming Challenges 
• 
For model drift: Implement robust monitoring with automated drift detection and retrain

question: What are the MLOps challenges?
answer:
Based on the context, the key MLOps challenges are:

1. **Model Drift** - Gradual degradation in model performance over time as real-world data distributions shift

2. **Data Inconsistencies** - Variations in data quality and distribution across environments and time periods

3. **Reproducibility Issues** - Difficulty in replicating experiments and results across team members and environments

4. **Deployment Delays** - Slow and complex model release processes caused by manual steps and inadequate tooling

5. **Lack of Collaboration** - Gaps and friction between data science, engineering, and operations teams

The context also notes that implementing MLOps at scale is not straightforward, and organizations face both technical and organizational challenges that require deliberate strategies to overcome.
